one_line_takeaway: vEcho turns LLM-assisted SAST verification into a closed, memory-driven agent loop (context-aware deep verification + Echoic Vulnerability Propagation) that on CWE-Bench-Java beats the IRIS baseline on both detection rate (65.00% vs 45.83%) and false-positive rate (59.78% vs 84.82% Avg FDR), and separately surfaced 51 real 0-days including Apache-confirmed RCE/ACE flaws.
key_finding: On the full 120-project CWE-Bench-Java benchmark (Table 2), vEcho (GPT-4.1) reached a 65.00% detection rate (78/120) versus IRIS's 45.83% (55/120, a 41.8% relative improvement) and versus SAST-Tools-Only (24.17%, 29/120) and Vanilla LLM (28.33%, 34/120). vEcho's Avg FDR was 59.78%, a 28.3% relative reduction from IRIS's 84.82% (SAST-Tools-Only: 94.16%; Vanilla LLM: 86.45%). Avg F1 was 0.422 for vEcho vs. 0.177 for IRIS, 0.063 for SAST-Tools-Only, and 0.220 for Vanilla LLM. Per-CWE breakdown (Figure 2/Section 4.3) showed 83.6% (46/55) detection on CWE-22 (Path Traversal). Beyond the labeled 120, vEcho surfaced 37 additional true vulnerabilities within the benchmark corpus not in the original ground truth, concentrated in CWE-502 (Deserialization) and CWE-22 (Path Traversal) per Figure 3, plus 51 novel 0-day vulnerabilities in other real-world open-source projects, including Critical RCE/ACE flaws officially confirmed by the Apache project. Ablation (Table 3): removing EVP dropped #Detected from 78 to 62 (Avg F1 0.422 -> 0.370), isolating EVP's contribution to recall; removing the Developer Tool Suite/Cognitive Memory dropped Avg F1 from 0.422 to 0.271 and raised Avg FDR from 59.78% to 84.97% (a 25.19-point jump, nearly matching the fully-ablated Vanilla LLM's 86.45%), isolating that component's contribution to precision/false-positive filtering. Base-model comparison (Table 1, 10-project subset) showed GPT-4.1 best overall (Avg FDR 52.94%, Avg F1 0.420), with smaller/weaker models (GLM-4.5, Qwen3 8B/30B) achieving comparable or higher detection counts but much worse Avg FDR (70.97%-89.47%), i.e., they over-call true positives rather than reasoning carefully.
core_contribution: vEcho, an LLM-agent framework for Java Web vulnerability auditing that reframes LLM-assisted SAST from passive alert verification to proactive vulnerability discovery. It has four stages: (1) Candidate Generation & Filtering, which runs CodeQL and Coverity in parallel with a self-developed LLM-based scanning agent (ScanAgent) that is not bound to predefined taint specifications, then deduplicates/merges and filters obviously irrelevant alerts; (2) Context-Aware Deep Verification, where a VerifyAgent drives a multi-turn analysis using a developer tool suite (code navigation, project comprehension, web search) instead of judging an isolated taint path; (3) Cognitive Learning & Feedback, a Cognitive Memory Module with a Temporary KB (project-specific working memory) and a Permanent KB (cross-project generalizable patterns), populated by a feedback loop after every TP/FP verification; and (4) Echoic Vulnerability Propagation (EVP), the paper's headline mechanism, which after each verification either issues 'Re-Scan Guidance' (new audit hypotheses distilled from an analysis, including from false positives) or, after a confirmed TP, performs similarity-driven search for code with the same dangerous calls/data-flow patterns or similar developer behavior; EVP outputs are routed to ScanAgent (not CodeQL/Coverity) for a secondary scan whose results feed back into Stage 1, forming an Analyze -> Learn -> Rediscover -> Re-analyze closed loop.
threat_model: Not an attacker-facing threat model; this is a defensive vulnerability-discovery/audit tool. The 'adversary' being defended against is the presence of unknown code-level vulnerabilities (path traversal CWE-22, OS command injection CWE-78, XSS CWE-79, deserialization/CWE-94 style code injection, and CWE-502 insecure deserialization) in Java web codebases. A stated non-trivial adversarial concern is information leakage to the LLM's own web-search tool: to prevent vEcho from looking up project names or CVE numbers and trivially 'knowing' the answer, the authors anonymized the CWE-Bench-Java dataset (stripped project names, .git history, pom.xml dependency names, and any code-comment metadata revealing CVEs) before evaluation. The paper also explicitly addresses dual-use/responsible-disclosure risk around the 0-days it found (Section 5.2): findings were privately reported via CVD to maintainers (including Apache), and vulnerability details in the paper are desensitized/obscured and included only with permission.
limitations: The paper's own Section 5.1 lists three limitations: (1) performance is deeply coupled to the base LLM's reasoning capability, and inherent hallucination/misunderstanding of complex logic can still cause misjudgments despite tool-grounded evidence; (2) the framework is claimed to be language-agnostic by design but the current instantiation and evaluation are Java-Web-only, so applicability to Python, C/C++, or non-standard project layouts is untested; (3) Deep Verification's multi-turn LLM calls and tool executions carry materially higher analysis overhead than traditional SAST, which may not scale to ultra-large codebases (e.g., the Linux kernel is given as an example). Additional issues evident from the text but not framed by the authors as limitations: IRIS's numbers are taken directly from its own paper rather than re-run under identical infrastructure/model access (the authors substitute GPT-4.1 for IRIS's original deprecated GPT-4-0125-Preview and argue this is the closest fair comparison, but it is still not an apples-to-apples re-implementation); the RQ1 base-model comparison used only a 10-project subsample, a small basis for model selection; and the FDR-computation choice to count the 37 newly-discovered-but-unlabeled true vulnerabilities as false positives, while conservative and disclosed, means the headline 59.78% Avg FDR is explicitly an undercount of vEcho's real precision.

datasets_used: CWE-Bench-Java (120 manually verified Java projects; CWE-22, CWE-78, CWE-79, CWE-94), originally released by the IRIS paper, anonymized by the authors (project names, .git history, pom.xml dependency names, CVE-revealing comments stripped) to prevent web-search leakage
10-project random subset of CWE-Bench-Java, used only for RQ1 base-model selection
Other real-world open-source Java projects (unnamed in the excerpted text) audited in the case study / 0-day discovery campaign, yielding 51 novel 0-days
quantitative_results: RQ2 full-benchmark (120 projects): vEcho 65.00% detection (78/120), Avg FDR 59.78%, Avg F1 0.422
RQ2: IRIS 45.83% detection (55/120), Avg FDR 84.82%, Avg F1 0.177
RQ2: SAST Tools Only 24.17% detection (29/120), Avg FDR 94.16%, Avg F1 0.063
RQ2: Vanilla LLM 28.33% detection (34/120), Avg FDR 86.45%, Avg F1 0.220
vEcho found 37 additional true vulnerabilities in CWE-Bench-Java beyond the 120 labeled, and 51 novel 0-days in other projects (incl. Apache-confirmed Critical RCE/ACE)

RQ3 ablation: vEcho full 78 detected / 65.00% / Avg FDR 59.78% / Avg F1 0.422; w/o EVP: 62 detected / 51.67% / Avg FDR 55.51% / Avg F1 0.370; w/o Tools & Memory: 49 detected / 40.83% / Avg FDR 84.97% / Avg F1 0.271; w/o both (=Vanilla LLM): 34 detected / 28.33% / Avg FDR 86.45% / Avg F1 0.220

RQ1 base-model comparison (10-project subset): GPT-4.1 6/10 detected, 60%, Avg FDR 52.94%, Avg F1 0.420; DeepSeek-R1-0528 5/10, 50%, Avg FDR 62.53%, Avg F1 0.417; GLM-4.5 6/10, 60%, Avg FDR 70.97%, Avg F1 0.325; Qwen3 30B 4/10, 40%, Avg FDR 89.47%, Avg F1 0.191; Qwen3 8B 6/10, 60%, Avg FDR 86.96%, Avg F1 0.222
baselines_compared: SAST Tools Only (raw CodeQL + Coverity output)
IRIS (SOTA LLM+CodeQL taint-specification/verification framework; metrics cited from the IRIS paper, originally using GPT-4-0125-Preview)
Vanilla LLM (LLM with no vEcho tool suite/memory/EVP, given the same 'vEcho Extended Input' candidate set as vEcho, to isolate vEcho's own agentic components)
vEcho w/o EVP and vEcho w/o Developer Tool Suite & Cognitive Memory (internal ablations, not external baselines)
